title: Day 23 Interview Prep — 8 Senior Architecture Questions
description: Senior-level discussion on architecture, performance, scalability, and maintainability. Answer out loud after your production refactor — defend every decision like a staff engineer review.

questions.question: Walk me through your refactored folder structure. Why did you organize it this way?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Can you articulate colocation, scalability, and onboarding benefits — not just copy a template.
questions.sampleAnswer: I moved to feature-based folders — features/products with components, hooks, api, types colocated. Shared atoms like Button live in components/ui. Routes in routes/. Code that changes together lives together, so a product feature refactor does not touch auth. Trade-off: more directories vs faster navigation for new engineers.
followUps: When would you split a feature further?

questions.question: You used Context in this project. Why not Redux? When would you migrate?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: State management judgment — Week 3 core decision with honest trade-offs.
questions.sampleAnswer: Context handles theme and auth snapshot — low-frequency reads, simple toggle actions. I would migrate to Redux when actions multiply, middleware is needed, or slices interact — cart + inventory + UI flags. RTK Query stays separate for server cache regardless. Context performance is fine here because updates are rare and value is memoized.

questions.question: Explain your performance optimizations. What did you apply and what did you skip?
questions.sampleAnswer: I profiled the product list — ProductCard re-rendered on unrelated filter state. Wrapped ProductCard in memo and stabilized onAdd with useCallback. useMemo on filtered list because 500 items filtered every keystroke. I skipped memo on layout shells — cheap renders. Lazy-loaded admin route — not needed on first paint. Documented each decision.

questions.question: How does your app scale if users grow from 100 to 100,000?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Scalability thinking beyond the demo — pagination, caching, code splitting.
questions.sampleAnswer: Client-side: route-level code splitting already done; virtualize long lists. Server-side: paginate API, RTK Query cache with tag invalidation prevents refetch storms. Auth tokens short-lived with refresh. Static assets on CDN. I would not load 10k products client-side — server pagination is the real fix.

questions.question: How do you maintain this codebase six months from now when requirements change?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Maintainability — types, tests, documentation, bounded features.
questions.sampleAnswer: Feature folders limit blast radius — new payment feature is features/payments, not scattered edits. TypeScript catches API contract drift. ARCHITECTURE.md explains state decisions so the next dev does not reverse them blindly. Content validation in CI for FrontForge-style JSON. I refactor incrementally — vertical slices, not big-bang.

questions.question: Why RTK Query here instead of manual fetch + useEffect?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Server state ownership — Week 3 retention under senior scrutiny.
questions.sampleAnswer: Products need caching, refetch on mutation, and deduplicated in-flight requests. RTK Query gives useGetProductsQuery with isLoading, isError, and invalidateTags on create/update. Manual fetch would reinvent cache maps and race conditions. Since I already have a Redux store for client UI state, RTK Query integrates cleanly.

questions.question: What accessibility improvements did you make in the production refactor?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Production readiness includes a11y — often skipped in demos.
questions.sampleAnswer: Replaced div onClick with button elements. Added htmlFor labels on all form inputs. Error messages use role=alert. On API failure, focus moves to retry button. Tab order follows visual order. Verified contrast on primary text. These are ship-blockers in production — not nice-to-haves.

questions.question: Name three trade-offs you made and three things you would improve with another sprint.
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Honest senior communication — imperfection with a plan.
questions.sampleAnswer: Trade-offs: (1) client-side filter vs server search — shipped faster but won't scale past 1k items; (2) Context for auth vs full Redux — simpler but harder to time-travel debug; (3) minimal test coverage vs refactor velocity. Next sprint: server pagination, Playwright E2E on checkout flow, error monitoring with Sentry. I would not add micro-frontends — over-engineering for this scale.
redFlags: Proposes rewrite instead of incremental fix
